Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".

Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X".     <fact>Supports three known backends: kiro-cli acp, npx claude-agent-acp, and npx @zed-industries/codex-acp</fact>

    <fact>buildSeedTemplate generates /agent init scaffold with current .env agent as guaranteed-valid entry</fact>

  <narrative>The telegram-kiro-bridge project implements a sophisticated multi-agent backend system allowing runtime switching between different AI providers (Kiro, Claude Code, Codex). The architecture uses a JSON configuration file (acp-providers.json) that stores per-machine backend definitions with their command lines, display labels, and optional model/effort pins. The system handles model pinning through session configuration options rather than CLI flags, specifically accommodating claude-agent-acp's requirement that model selection occur via session/set_config_option after session initialization. The parser intelligently adds --agent flags to Kiro commands when ACP_AGENT_NAME is set, and provides graceful degradation to a default backend mirroring the .env configuration when the config file is missing or invalid. The seed template generator creates an initialization scaffold for /agent init that includes the current .env agent as a known-working entry plus scaffolds for other common backends.</narrative>

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Model and effort pinning implementation via session/set_config_option</title>
  <subtitle>Best-effort configuration system applies model before effort after session creation, with failure tolerance for adapter compatibility</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>applyModelEffort method executes after session/new completes and sessionId is assigned</fact>
    <fact>acpModel parameter accepts canonical model IDs or fuzzy aliases like "fable-5"</fact>
    <fact>acpEffort parameter requires exact match to model-supported values (low/medium/high), no fuzzy fallback</fact>
    <fact>Model pin applied before effort pin because adapter rebuilds effort option list from selected model</fact>
    <fact>Configuration failures logged to stderr but swallowed to prevent session startup abortion</fact>
    <fact>Only claude-agent-acp adapter currently honours model config option</fact>
    <fact>Ephemeral sessions (scheduler, specialist, daily-intel, session-extract) skip model/effort pinning</fact>
    <fact>Main interactive session exclusively uses model/effort pinning feature</fact>
    <fact>isTransientBackendError detects retryable backend failures like dispatch failures, timeouts, and connection resets</fact>
    <fact>Prompt retry system defaults to 2 retries with 1000ms base backoff, doubled per attempt</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The AcpClient implements a sophisticated model and effort pinning system that operates in a best-effort, non-blocking manner. After a new session is created and the sessionId is received, the applyModelEffort method attempts to configure the session's model and reasoning effort through the session/set_config_option ACP request. The system applies the model pin first, then the effort pin, because effort levels are model-specific and the adapter must rebuild its effort options based on the selected model. Any failures during this configuration phase—whether from unsupported adapters, invalid values, or stream errors—are logged but do not abort the session startup, ensuring the bridge remains operational even with misconfigured pinning. This feature is intentionally limited to the main interactive session; ephemeral background sessions like schedulers and specialists skip pinning entirely to preserve their existing behavior. The client also implements automatic retry logic for transient backend errors (AWS dispatch failures, connection resets, timeouts) with exponential backoff, defaulting to 2 retries starting at 1000ms.</narrative>

    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>

  </files_read>

</observation>

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Provider creation and backend switching architecture</title>
  <subtitle>Backend switching uses wholesale replacement (not fallback) for model/effort and employs full archive pipeline for conversation handoff</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>createProvider method instantiates AcpProvider with backend-specific or default config</fact>
    <fact>When backend is pinned via /agent, its model/effort values wholesale replace .env defaults</fact>
    <fact>Empty backend.model or backend.effort leaves adapter's own default untouched, does NOT fall back to config.acpModel/acpEffort</fact>
    <fact>ACP session resume enabled via config.acpSessionResume flag with registry persistence</fact>
    <fact>saveResumable records sessionId, backendKey, agentCommand, cwd, and savedAt timestamp for crash recovery</fact>
    <fact>switchAgent method reuses full drop() close pipeline including archiveOnClose and onBeforeClose handlers</fact>
    <fact>archiveOnClose performs substantive guard check, carries forward priorArchive, and generates LLM summary</fact>
    <fact>onBeforeClose saves transcript and working-state before switch completes</fact>
    <fact>New session's create() finds archived context on disk and injects handoff into preamble</fact>
    <fact>switchAgent throws error if turn is in-flight or backend key unknown</fact>
    <fact>DirectProvider created for non-ACP provider types with model/baseUrl/apiKey from config</fact>
    <fact>Local model provider filters MCP servers to memory, notebooklm, and google due to limited context</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The sessionManager implements a sophisticated provider creation and switching system that treats backend configuration as wholesale replacement rather than fallback-based inheritance. When a backend is selected via /agent, its model and effort values completely replace the environment defaults—if the backend defines no model, the system leaves the adapter's own default untouched rather than falling back to config.acpModel. This design choice ensures each backend operates with its intended configuration independently. The ACP session resume feature persists session metadata (sessionId, backendKey, agentCommand, cwd, savedAt) to a registry when config.acpSessionResume is enabled, allowing recovery even after complete bridge crashes. Backend switching employs the full drop() close pipeline: archiveOnClose performs substantive conversation checks and generates LLM summaries, onBeforeClose saves transcripts and working state, and the subsequent create() call discovers this archived context and injects it as handoff preamble for seamless conversation continuity. The system prevents switches during in-flight turns to maintain consistency.</narrative>

The user is showing me grep results about session resuming functionality in acpClient.ts. This reveals:

1. There's a `resumeSessionId` parameter in the initialize method
2. When `resumeSessionId` is provided and the agent has `loadSession` capability, it tries to call `session/load`
3. If `session/load` succeeds, it returns `resumed: true` and SKIPS `applyModelEffort`
4. If `session/load` fails, it falls back to `session/new` and DOES call `applyModelEffort`
5. There are suppression flags for session/load replay to avoid duplicate token processing

This is an important discovery about why applyModelEffort is skipped on resume - because the resumed session already has its model/effort configured from before, and trying to reconfigure it would be wrong.

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Session resumption flow and model pinning skip logic</title>
  <subtitle>Resumed sessions skip applyModelEffort because configuration already exists from prior session state</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>initialize method accepts resumeSessionId parameter for session restoration</fact>
    <fact>When resumeSessionId provided and agent has loadSession capability, system calls session/load</fact>
    <fact>Successful session/load returns resumed:true and skips applyModelEffort call</fact>
    <fact>Failed session/load falls back to session/new and executes applyModelEffort normally</fact>
    <fact>session/load replay suppression prevents duplicate session/update events during history replay</fact>
    <fact>Suppression flag prevents redundant log emissions and token-counting side effects</fact>
    <fact>Resumed session reuses original sessionId and creates new AcpTracer with that ID</fact>
    <fact>Model and effort configuration preserved from original session, no reconfiguration needed</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The AcpClient implements a sophisticated session resumption system that intelligently skips model and effort reconfiguration when loading a previously-saved session. When a resumeSessionId is provided and the agent declares loadSession capability, the system attempts to restore the session via session/load rather than creating a fresh one. On successful resume, applyModelEffort is intentionally skipped because the loaded session already contains its model and effort configuration from the original session state—reapplying these settings would be redundant and potentially disruptive. If session/load fails for any reason, the system gracefully falls back to creating a new session via session/new and applies the current model/effort configuration normally. The resumption mechanism also includes suppression logic to prevent duplicate session/update events during history replay, avoiding redundant log emissions and token-counting side effects that would occur if the system treated replayed turns as new activity.</narrative>

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Session resume validation and candidate resolution flow</title>
  <subtitle>Resume candidate validated against agent command and backend key before passing to provider initialization</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>session-registry.js exports loadResumable, saveResumable, clearResumable, listSessions, setActive, clearActive, renameSession functions</fact>
    <fact>resolveResumeCandidate validates resume eligibility based on config.acpSessionResume flag</fact>
    <fact>Resume candidate must match both agentCommand and backend key (backend?.key ?? "") from registry record</fact>
    <fact>resumeSessionId resolved only for ACP provider type, undefined for DirectProvider sessions</fact>
    <fact>resumeSessionId flows from resolveResumeCandidate through createProvider to AcpProvider constructor</fact>
    <fact>Mismatched agent command or backend key causes resume to be skipped, returns undefined</fact>
    <fact>Session registry enforces MAX_SESSIONS_PER_CHAT limit for session tracking</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The sessionManager implements a strict validation pipeline for session resumption that ensures consistency between saved state and current configuration. When creating a new session, the system calls resolveResumeCandidate which checks if session resumption is enabled via config.acpSessionResume, then loads the saved registry record for the chat. The resume is only permitted if both the agent command line and backend key exactly match the values recorded when the session was saved—this prevents attempting to resume a claude-fable-5 session with a different model or a Kiro session with Claude. If validation passes, the sessionId flows through createProvider to the AcpProvider constructor where it triggers the session/load attempt. If validation fails at any point (wrong command, wrong backend, or no saved record), resumeSessionId remains undefined and a fresh session is created instead. This strict matching ensures resumed sessions maintain their original configuration and prevents subtle bugs from configuration drift.</narrative>

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Session registry persistence structure and management</title>
  <subtitle>Registry stores multiple session records per chat in acp-session-registry.json with active session tracking and automatic migration</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Session registry persisted to config/acp-session-registry.json in MEMORY_DIR</fact>
    <fact>Registry structure maps chatId to chat record containing activeId and sessions array</fact>
    <fact>activeId field points to currently resumable session ID, empty string means no active session</fact>
    <fact>Each chat can track multiple SessionRecord entries up to MAX_SESSIONS_PER_CHAT limit</fact>
    <fact>SessionRecord includes id, backendKey, agentCommand, cwd, and savedAt timestamp fields</fact>
    <fact>clearResumable filters out active session and deletes entire chat entry when sessions array becomes empty</fact>
    <fact>listSessions returns activeId and sessions array for given chatId</fact>
    <fact>renameSession finds session by id within chat's sessions array</fact>
    <fact>Shape detection provides automatic migration when reading legacy registry formats</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The session-registry module implements a multi-session tracking system persisted to acp-session-registry.json. Each chat maintains a collection of saved session records, with one designated as "active" via the activeId pointer—this is the session that will be resumed when create() is called. The structure allows multiple saved sessions per chat up to the MAX_SESSIONS_PER_CHAT limit, enabling users to maintain different session contexts (e.g., different backend configurations or work streams) and switch between them. When a session is cleared via clearResumable, only the active session is removed from the array; if this leaves the array empty, the entire chat entry is deleted from the registry to keep the file clean. The registry includes automatic migration logic that detects the stored shape format and upgrades legacy structures when reading, ensuring backward compatibility as the system evolves.</narrative>

  <narrative>The claude-agent-acp adapter implements a sophisticated three-stage fuzzy matching algorithm for resolving model preferences. When getAvailableModels is called, it reads settings.model and attempts to resolve it against the SDK's model list using resolveModelPreference. The resolution tries exact matches first (checking both canonical IDs and display names case-insensitively), then falls back to bidirectional substring matching (allowing "opus" to match "claude-opus-4-6" or vice versa), and finally employs tokenized scoring for complex aliases like "opus[1m]". The resolved model becomes the currentModel and is set via query.setModel() before the function returns. If no match is found, the adapter defaults to the first model in the SDK's list (models[0]), which could explain unexpected model selection when the requested model isn't recognized.</narrative>

This explains why users see opus-4.6 being used despite configuring Fable 5 in acp-providers.json. <observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>setSessionConfigOption error handling explains silent fallback behavior</title>
  <subtitle>Invalid model names throw errors caught by bridge's applyModelEffort, causing silent fallback to SDK's default model</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>setSessionConfigOption validates incoming model value against session.configOptions list</fact>
    <fact>For configId="model", handler first attempts exact match then falls back to resolveModelPreference</fact>
    <fact>When resolveModelPreference returns null for unrecognized model, validValue remains undefined</fact>
    <fact>Handler throws "Invalid value for config option model: {value}" error when validValue is null</fact>
    <fact>Bridge's applyModelEffort catches exception and logs "[acp] set model={value} not applied (ignored)" to stderr</fact>
    <fact>Session continues with default model set during getAvailableModels initialization (models[0])</fact>
    <fact>Error message written to stderr but session startup never aborts per best-effort design</fact>
    <fact>Users see opus-4.6 without realizing Fable config was rejected due to missed stderr log</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The complete error handling flow explains why users see opus-4.6 instead of their configured Fable 5 without receiving clear feedback. When the bridge calls session/set_config_option with "claude-fable-5", the adapter's setSessionConfigOption handler validates the model name by first checking for an exact match in the available models list, then falling back to resolveModelPreference for fuzzy matching. Since the SDK 0.2.83 model catalog lacks Fable definitions, resolveModelPreference returns null, causing validValue to remain undefined. The handler then throws "Invalid value for config option model: claude-fable-5". However, the bridge's applyModelEffort method catches this exception per its best-effort design philosophy and merely logs "[acp] set model=claude-fable-5 not applied (ignored)" to stderr without aborting session startup. The session continues running with the default model that was set during getAvailableModels initialization—which defaults to models[0] (opus-4.6) when no prior persisted settings exist. Users miss this failure because stderr messages are easily overlooked in Telegram chat interfaces, creating the illusion that model pinning is silently ignored rather than explicitly rejected.</narrative>
